sentences.text: My name is Jean Melzer, J-E-A-N M-E-L-Z-E-R.
F-I-N-K, Fink.
May 10, 1923. 72, 73-- I don't know, who count?
[LAUGHS]
May 10.
It's over.
I was born in Zawalow.
And I had--
Yes.
Z-A-W-A-L-O-W.
We had a very comfortable home.
I had a wonderful childhood.
Everything was very good for us.
My parents had a business, grocery.
And I had a younger brother.
And we, we lived very good there.
And--
Yes.
Until-- we used to have together-- to belong to organizations, to go on all kinds of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to get together from other villages, from other cities.
And we had a wonderful life until the war start.
And anti-Semitism start a little bit before the war.
We first lived out of the town.
And they made us to leave this house.
And we had to go into the center of the town.
We bought another house.
And we continued with the business.
They used to break our windows.
That was in 193-- '36 or '37.
Seven--
I'm--
I think I was like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 12 or 13 years.
I was then in another town and school.
Because in our town, it wasn't-- I finished in our town.
It was a small town.
So I went to another town to school.
And when I came home for the vacation, so I was told how it was, that the, the windows were broken.
And they sent us a note that we have to leave the town till-- the house.
Till three days, we had to leave it.
My parents bought another house in the center of the town.
And we continued with the, with the life.
It wasn't-- it was still very, very good till 1939, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the real war start.
Yes.
Yes.
We were observers of the holidays, all the holidays.
We had a kosher home.
We, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we belonged to organizations, to the Zionist groups.
We had, we had, like, rented a, a place.
And all the youth used to come there.
And we were there, making all kind of speeches.
And, and we had a very happy life there like this.
One.
One brother, a younger one.
He was three years younger than I am.
He was 14 years when he was killed.
What?
Can I take the paper?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] glasses.
Yeah.
'40-- '41, '41, [INAUDIBLE], yeah.
My father's name was [?
Joseph. ?] And my mother's name was Rachela, Rachela.
Gottesman.
He was a successful businessman.
We were-- and they would always working, busy.
And we continued school, playing, going with our friends, were Gentiles.
And we were very friendly, very-- when a holiday was a Jewish or a-- we invited them.
We played together.
And we always were very friendly.
We went to school together, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until, until the war.
In '36, it all-- it was all starting, like-- but, like, we were young.
And we still didn't feel so.
Because the parents felt more.
And we still lived, till '39, very happily.
And in '39, when the war real start, then it begin to be worst.
We, we had to give up some kind of business.
Our friends started to be a little bit different to us.
But still, we continued our, our life normally.
Yeah, I went to--
No, I went to a regular school, a public school, and-- like high school.
And afternoon, I used to go to [?
Bes ?] [?
Yankov ?].
So I was teached both in Jewish, in Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] history and, and in there [? words ?] education.
The Jewish people were always for education.
I remember in our home, we always had private teachers.
And I studied German even, because we, we used to love the German language.
And we used to think that they are such a intelligent-- no, not nature.
How would you say-- intelligent people that it cannot be that they shall do this, what we heard when Hitler start coming.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I went, when I finished high school, I start studying the bookkeeping.
And I went to school in [INAUDIBLE].
But I was a short time there.
And I had to come back home, because it was very bad when the Germans [INAUDIBLE] that's--
Yeah.
That's still before the war, right?
In '39, we were in Poland, yeah.
We were all the time in Poland.
I mean--
Don't ?] [? recall. ?]
Yes.
Yes.
It will be, like, in '39-- mhm, '39, '40, '39.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My-- we had family there in the city.
We were surrounded always with friends, aunts, uncles.
My mother had three brothers and two sisters.
They lived in other towns.
But my father had two sisters.
They lived in the same town.
I had cousins and we-- and friends there.
We were very happy together.
Yes.
Yes.
Yeah, like Passover, we used to have together.
My, my father had two sisters.
So at one time, yeah, we were living already in the center of the town there, where we bought another house.
And it was Passover, that the stores were closed.
And at this time, we had a house there that we rented out to another, to a Gentile.
And a policeman was living there.
And they came to break the windows.
It was--
No, this was still the Poles.
They-- and instead to break our window, because it was closed, like, with locks.
Because when the store was closed and the entrance in this place was closed, because it was a holiday.
So those windows were to the open from this apartment.
They broke his window.
And he ran out.
And he catched one.
It was a Ukranian.
And then they-- this was still in time when Poland was so-- that's when we start feeling the, the war is near there, like when Hitler came to the-- in 1933.
So everything used to change.
So on the year that they broke the windows.
And we were not touched then.
But we were scared.
And it interrupted our seder, that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we felt it's not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that maybe we, we have to try to, to leave this Poland, to run.
But then we start again.
There were-- there, there are our roots.
Our grandparents will, will lived there.
And all our families are there.
Why shall we leave?
But we, we felt that we need a Jewish, a land that we shall have where to go to.
That's when we were belonging to the organizations.
We, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we tried to collect money for Israel and all those things.
Not to Israel-- my parents were thinking of going to the United States.
Because my mother and father were-- when they were single, they were in the United States.
They came back before the, the war start, the first war, 1914.
Not so seriously, but they were thinking of it.
Yeah.
Well, the town of the Jewish community wasn't big.
It was small.
We had, like, 30 Jewish families.
The rest was everything Gentile.
It was a small town.
But we lived very friendly with the, the Gentile people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have, I had friends.
And we grew up together.
When, when they had a holiday, they invited us.
We couldn't participate in everything what they do on according to our kosher.
So we ate fruit or other things.
And they were invited to our holidays.
We lived always friendly with them.
Yeah, in a house.
Mhm.
Yeah.
The house was in the center of the town.
We had a store.
And it had, like, four entrances.
In the front were two entrances, one to the store, the other one to our house-- to the building, to the room, which was only three rooms.
But the rooms were very large.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Magazine.
Yeah.
No, not receipts-- products, like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had, let's say, you bags of rice--
Flour, this very--
The inventory?
Whatever it is.
Yeah.
Well, we had-- no, I didn't have my own room in the beginning.
We had-- because we rented.
When we bought the house, this policeman was there living.
So that's why we couldn't.
And we tried, he shall move out.
And then we had the other rooms, too.
But me and my brother had a separate- like, it was divided, like a separate room.
Yeah.
Zalman, Zalman, a Jewish name.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
When he heard, he ran out right away.
He ran-- he thought-- we were screaming.
We heard such a noise.
So-- and the policeman thought they, they in the store.
He ran in through the house to the store and opened the door and ran after them.
And he, he catched them.
No, maybe '37.
Yeah.
But it still went in.
A lot of, they said, don't buy by Jews and don't go to-- with Jews.
But it quiet down again.
And we had our business still up.
And then we lived OK till '39.
And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in '39 also, when the war start, the-- Poland was like-- the Germans came in.
And the Russians came in.
So we were till 1941, until the, until the Russians, which was also OK.
The-- it wasn't as good as it used to be before the war start.
But we, we continued in living normally until '41, when the war start.
Will you take this?
I must check.
What was it you're asking?
When the Russians came in, we were considered as that we are, we are rich.
My father owned land.
And we were storekeepers.
So they, they were more for, Like, you should be poorer people.
But still, we, we didn't have no more our store.
And because my father gave up the store, we had a lot of land.
We--
We had to then-- in our store, they put up another store, and not a grocery no more.
And we worked there, as working people.
No, they didn't take it away.
But we discontinued the grocery.
And we just had, the like, other, other things.
The, the Russians took the-- and put like-- it was-- that was-- I really don't want to go into this.
Can we start from this?
From ?] Russia.
Yeah.
Let's leave.
[INAUDIBLE] Russia with this.
When we were in-- at home in Zawalow, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the Germans came in, we had-- they took all those people to work.
And the first day, when they just came, they started to ask where the Jewish people lived.
And the Gentiles, the Polish people who were there, they right away brought them into the Jewish places where they were.
They were very happy to see them.
They-- when they came in, they were happy.
They screamed, [?
Hora ?], [?
Hora ?], to see them.
And they showed right away the Jewish places.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They start taking us to work, to all kind of work.
I had three houses away from us, a friend who went to school together with me.
And he joined the Nazis.
Yes.
Yeah, [?
Romich. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He joined the Nazis a little later.
But he was pro them right away.
And he, he took my father to work, and other people.
They cut off their beards.
And they, they took them to, to ditch, like street work, to do all kinds of dirty works.
They took them, like when it was lot rain or mud, they had to lay down, put them on the floor, and get up, lay down, and get up, just to humiliate them.
They shall be-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and beaten.
And they took them to dig stones.
We have-- like our city was-- now I go back to this, how the city was.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They had-- it was surrounded like with-- it was in a, in a vale, or around was hills.
The hills had stones.
So we had to go dig those stones.
They took the men specially.
And they beat them.
And they took them there to work.
He, this [?
Romich, ?] specially, he was very bad to them.
The women also was taking to-- took to work.
But they took them, like, to do their houseworks, to wash their clothes, to, to clean toilets-- they-- and-- by everything, they were beating us.
Me, yeah, specially the young ones more than my mother.
They used to took, to take us to field work that-- daily.
And this work was unnecessarily.
But they took us.
Because they wanted just to humiliate us and to, to beat us, and to make us work for them.
Yeah, when they, not-- they had, like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had-- they-- like-- what was it?
The, the, like city hall.
And that, they took us to clean out those houses and put them privately where they were living.
The-- my mother was at home.
Her, they didn't take to this work at this time, no.
Yeah.
My brother was taken to work, too.
Also to this kind of work, [INAUDIBLE] they-- to sweep the streets, to, to wash, to wash the, the sidewalks, the-- such things.
No, in '41 when--
